coredata.dc:title: Experimental investigation of high temperature organic phase change materials for waste heat recovery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Righetti, Giulia
Guarda, Dario
Doretti, Luca
Zilio, Claudio
Martelletto, Francesca
Dolfi, Andrea
Travaglini, Giuseppe
Mancin, Simone
coredata.dc:description: This paper compares the performance of two high temperature organic phase change materials during solid/liquid phase change to study the effects of 3D printed periodic structures in enhancing the heat transfer performance. The experimental results reveal that erythritol and an organic material, P130 respond differently to the same thermal stimuli, emphasizing the importance of this analysis to highlight the underpinning heat and mass transfer mechanisms. It can be stated that the phase change materials present peculiar heat transfer characteristics and one of the main challenges is represented by the difficulty in identify a general thermal behaviour (latent heat storage capacity, heat transfer characteristics, material’s response to possible enhancement strategies, etc.) even within the same family of materials. This work confirms that the design of the latent thermal energy storage must be tailored around the specific application and that the thermal conductivity of the phase change materials does not deeply affect the need of the use of enhanced surfaces. In fact, only enhanced surfaces can permit to design efficient and effective latent thermal energy storages that meet the heat load demands.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PCM
Thermal energy storage
Phase change materials
Additive manufacturing
Periodic structures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DSC measurements of PCM phase change temperature and latent heat.
Sample design to maintain the same porosity. From left to right: reference (sample “r”), 40 mm (sample “4″), 20 mm (sample “2″), and 10 mm (sample “1”) base samples.
A picture of the four samples. From left to right: reference (sample “r”), 40 mm (sample “4″), 20 mm (sample “2″), and 10 mm (sample “1”) base samples.
Scheme of the experimental test rig and of the thermocouple locations.
Average temperature profiles of the PCM inside the 10 mm base size sample as the electric power input increases. (a) P130, (b) Erythritol.
Average temperature profile of the PCM inside during the 50 W test in the four samples. (a) organic PCM, (b) Erythritol.
Average temperature profile of the PCM inside during the 50 W test in the two materials.
Local temperature of the PCM inside during the 50 W test in the P130. (a) 10 mm sample, (b) 40 mm sample.
Local temperature of the PCM inside during the 50 W test in the Erythritol. (a) 10 mm sample, (b) 40 mm sample.
Average temperature profile of the PCM inside during the discharging test in the four samples. (a) organic PCM, (b) Erythritol.
PCM thermophysical properties: Organic PCM data from the manufacturer, Erytrhitol data from [39,40].
Main geometrical characteristics of the tested samples.

head.ce:title: Experimental investigation of high temperature organic phase change materials for waste heat recovery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Management and Engineering, University of Padova, Vicenza, IT, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Management and Engineering, University of Padova, Vicenza, IT, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil, Environmental and Architectural Engineering – ICEA, University of Padova, Padova, IT, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Civil, Environmental and Architectural Engineering – ICEA, University of Padova, Padova, IT, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Group Research and Technology, PETRONAS, Via Trinità 82, Santena, 10026, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cGroup Research and Technology, PETRONAS, Via Trinità 82, Santena, 10026, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper compares the performance of two high temperature organic phase change materials during solid/liquid phase change to study the effects of 3D printed periodic structures in enhancing the heat transfer performance.

The experimental results reveal that erythritol and an organic material, P130 respond differently to the same thermal stimuli, emphasizing the importance of this analysis to highlight the underpinning heat and mass transfer mechanisms. It can be stated that the phase change materials present peculiar heat transfer characteristics and one of the main challenges is represented by the difficulty in identify a general thermal behaviour (latent heat storage capacity, heat transfer characteristics, material’s response to possible enhancement strategies, etc.) even within the same family of materials. This work confirms that the design of the latent thermal energy storage must be tailored around the specific application and that the thermal conductivity of the phase change materials does not deeply affect the need of the use of enhanced surfaces. In fact, only enhanced surfaces can permit to design efficient and effective latent thermal energy storages that meet the heat load demands.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Decarbonization is a key priority for the European Union, as outlined in the European Green Deal, which aims for climate neutrality by 2050 and a 55 % reduction in greenhouse gas emissions by 2030 compared to 1990 levels [1].

Waste heat recovery is a crucial strategy for advancing decarbonization and enhancing energy efficiency in industrial processes. In many sectors, a significant portion of the energy supplied to production systems is lost as unused heat, leading to economic and environmental drawbacks. Industries such as aluminum low-pressure die casting [2], steel manufacturing, cement production [3], and ceramic tile manufacturing, pulp and paper, food processing generate substantial amounts of residual heat, often at high temperatures, which could be recovered and reused. Moreover, it has to be underlined that around 70 % of the total waste industrial energy, or 642 TWh/year, is low-temperature heat <300 °C of which 183 TWh/year is wasted at stream temperatures between 100 °C-200 °C [4]. Notably, a substantial portion of industrial waste heat is available at low temperatures, particularly below 150 °C, which is often underutilized. For instance, a study by Forman et al. [5] estimates that approximately 63 % of global waste heat streams arise at temperatures below 100 °C, indicating a vast potential for recovery in this low-temperature range. Additionally, research by Bianchi et al. [6] highlights that in the European Union, the theoretical potential for industrial waste heat recovery amounts to 920 TWh per year, with a Carnot potential of 279 TWh per year.

The implementation of waste heat recovery technologies, including Organic Rankine Cycles (ORCs) [7], sometimes combined with Phase Change Materials (PCMs) [8], thermoelectric generators [9], and latent thermal storages [10,11], not only can reduce fossil fuel consumption and CO₂ emissions but also decreases operational costs, improving both sustainability and competitiveness [10,12].

As described in literature, the PCMs can be subdivided in three main categories: organics, inorganics, and eutectics (i.e. mixtures of the previous categories). While inorganics can withstand extremely high temperature, the organic materials are not stable and can incur thermal degradation at temperature >200 °C. However, there is a great interest in studying organic PCM with a temperature below 150 °C, which can be suitable to recover the large amount of heat wasted below 200 °C, as mentioned above.

In these temperature ranges, various types of organic PCMs are available, each with distinct advantages and drawbacks that make them more or less suitable for specific applications [13]. As a result, the thermophysical properties of PCMs can vary significantly, influencing their charging and discharging characteristics and how they behave when subjected to thermal conductivity enhancements. This complicates the material selection for specific applications. Unfortunately, studies offering direct comparisons between different PCMs under the same operating conditions remain limited. This is particularly relevant for designers who must choose the most suitable PCM for a given thermal management problem—such as storing industrial waste heat below 150 °C—based not only on the phase change temperature, but also on other key properties such as latent heat, thermal conductivity, specific heat, etc. It is often assumed that materials with similar phase change temperatures will perform similarly, but this is not necessarily the case. Furthermore, the influence of these thermophysical properties on the effectiveness of thermal conductivity enhancement techniques—such as the addition of metallic particles or structures—has not been systematically studied. Understanding how such enhancements interact with the base properties of each PCM is essential for optimizing performance and guiding material selection in practical applications.

In the literature, there are a few case studies addressing this topic. For example, one study analyzed the main differences between paraffin and salt hydrate PCMs using the DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry) technique [14]. Another experimental investigation compared the heat transfer performance of two commercially available paraffinic PCMs (RT50 vs. RT44HC) in finned-tube heat exchangers for latent thermal energy storage systems [15]. A further study, compared the solar still performance utilizing different PCMs, by examining an inorganic PCM (capric–palmitic acid) and an organic PCM (A48), both with similar phase change temperatures. In that work, A48 was found to increase productivity by 92 % compared to a conventional solar still [16]. However, this last case is one of the few examples in which two different families of materials with similar phase change temperatures were directly compared in terms of thermal performance.

Consequently, it is common to generalize PCM behavior, assuming uniform performance across all materials without considering differences in composition and structure. Differently, to optimize the application of PCMs in advanced, efficient, and optimized latent thermal energy storage, it is essential to thoroughly understand their characteristics and assess their impact on overall system performance.

Erythritol has been widely identified as a potential PCM for applications such as solar water heating [17], domestic hot water systems [18], and waste heat recovery [19]. However, some studies have reported significant issues, including high subcooling, degradation of melting enthalpy—particularly due to oxygen exposure [20]—and stability concerns [21]. Despite its relatively high thermal conductivity compared to other organic PCMs, erythritol is still rarely used in industrial applications due to the above-mentioned limitations [22].

Various studies have explored its use in different thermal energy storage configurations. Coccia et al. [23] investigated erythritol within the annular volume of a double-wall stainless steel vessel Thermal Energy Storage (TES) containing 2.5 kg of material. Patel et al. [24] examined a 2.3 kg erythritol-based shell-and-tube heat storage system but noted that its thermal properties degraded after 200 cycles. To improve stability and prevent air exposure, Raymundo Junior et al. [25] used erythritol in spheres of 12 mm and 7 mm diameters subjected to external flow. Similarly, Nazzi Hems et al. [26] employed erythritol in spheres with diameters ranging from 10 mm to 40 mm, while Soni et al. [27] incorporated erythritol PCM with a 2.5 % copper nano-additive volume fraction in 100 mm diameter capsules within a flowing fluid system. Yang et al. [28] found that blending erythritol with expanded graphite could enhance stability while improving thermal conductivity.

In addition, the literature contains even more extensive studies on other, non-sugar alcohol, organic PCMs, particularly in waste heat recovery applications [29], as they exhibit excellent stability, repeatability, material compatibility, low subcooling, and high latent heat storage capacity [30]. On the other hand, one well-known drawback of organic PCMs is their low thermal conductivity, necessitating strategies such as the integration of metallic structures to enhance effective thermal conductivity and allow to couple the heat transfer performance with the demand. Numerous studies, particularly review articles, have documented and analyzed these approaches. For instance, Lin et al. [31] list a wide range of strategies for thermal conductivity enhancement; Rathore et al. [32] focus on solutions applicable to building applications; Moldgy et al. [33] specifically address techniques for waste heat recovery systems; while Paul et al. [34] analyze enhancement methods tailored to PCMs used in medium-temperature applications, such as solar thermal energy harvesting.

In this study, two different medium-high temperature organic PCMs (phase change temperature around 130 °C) with comparable phase change temperatures (around 128 vs. around 120 °C, see Table 1 ), both suitable in the field of waste heat recovery at medium-high temperature (for instance: waste heat recovery, solar water heating, Organic Rankine Cycles, etc.) were experimentally compared. The first material is an organic PCM (P130) which exhibits interesting properties as compared to the ones available on the market. In fact, P130 is a bio-based, non-paraffinic, non-flammable organic PCM developed by Petronas; although its exact composition is proprietary, its thermophysical properties were internally characterized and are reported in this study. This organic PCM was compared with erythritol, a naturally occurring polyol found in fruits, mushrooms, and fermented foods, that has gained attention as an eco-friendly PCM.

It provides a useful benchmark for performance comparison with P130. Despite the comparable melting point, erythritol differs significantly from P130 in its thermal conductivity, degree of supercooling, and latent heat—making it an ideal counterpart for evaluating how differences in thermophysical properties influence heat transfer behavior. In fact, the aim of this study is to investigate how their thermophysical properties can affect their final heat transfer performance in terms of charging and discharging times and temperature distribution inside the thermal storage. Furthermore, samples containing metal structures with the same amount of aluminum but different geometries were tested to determine if there is an optimal structure to enhance the heat transfer performance of the two analyzed PCMs (in terms of both charging time reduction and internal temperature uniformity), and whether this optimal structure is the same for both materials.

The pyramidal structures selected for this study are symmetric and periodic, enabling consistent geometric control and facilitating numerical modeling. This choice was informed by previous experimental works by the same research group indicating that optimal enhancement geometry can vary between PCMs [35–38]. The periodic nature of the design also ensured consistent metallic mass and PCM volume across samples, isolating the effect of geometry on thermal performance. Cell base sizes of 10, 20, and 40 mm were selected as a compromise between the maximum allowable size fitting within the 40 mm cubic sample and the minimum size manufacturable with the available 3D printing technology. In particular, cell sizes below 5 mm would have produced excessively thin ligaments, prone to structural instability or printing defects.

In brief, the aim of the study is to:
1.
Compare two medium-high temperature organic PCMs (P130 and erythritol), both with phase change temperatures around 130 °C and suitable for applications such as waste heat recovery, solar thermal systems, and Organic Rankine Cycles.
2.
Assess how the thermophysical properties of the two PCMs influence their heat transfer performance, particularly in terms of charging/discharging times and internal temperature distribution during thermal cycles.
3.
Investigate the effect of metallic structure geometry on thermal performance by testing different aluminum-enhanced samples with identical metallic mass but varying cell sizes (10, 20, and 40 mm).
4.
Determine whether an optimal metallic geometry (in terms of both charging time reduction and internal temperature uniformity) exists to enhance the heat transfer efficiency of each PCM, and assess if this optimal geometry is consistent across both materials.

Two different PCMs were selected for comparison, chosen because they present a comparable melting point (around 128 °C vs. around 120 °C, see Table 1), but different thermal conductivity, 0.15 W m-1 K-1 for the first and 0.6 W m-1 K-1 for the second. These two materials were selected because, although other options such as paraffin waxes, stearic acid (in eutectic blends), phenyl salicylate (salol), diphenyl ether, dimethyl terephthalate (DMT), and inorganic eutectic salts like LiNO₃–KCl are available, they combine high latent heat with non-toxicity, non-flammability, and biodegradability—making them particularly suitable for safe and sustainable thermal energy storage applications.

The first PCM, named P130, is an innovative organic material developed and produced by Petronas; it is stable, not toxic, nor harmful to health, neither corrosive. Its thermal energy storage properties, presented in Fig. 1 , were provided by the manufacturer based on experimental tests conducted on a small sample using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) at a scanning rate of 1 K min-1. During melting, a phase change temperature peak of 128.5 °C and a latent heat of 220.1 kJ kg-1 were recorded, while during solidification, the peak temperature was 134.56 °C with a latent heat of 225.8 kJ kg-1. Additionally, the material has a density of 1200 kg m-³, with a specific heat capacity of 2.06 kJ kg-1 K-1 at 100 °C and 2.92 kJ kg-1 K-1 at 160 °C. The DSC analysis of the P130 is reported in Fig. 1.

The second PCM is the erythritol supplied by Smartlab Company, a naturally occurring polyol found in fruits, mushrooms, and fermented foods. Erythritol has gained attention as an eco-friendly PCM, despite some concerns regarding long-term stability over multiple cycles [22]. With a melting point of approximately 119 °C and a latent heat of 333–344 kJ/kg, erythritol can be considered a promising candidate for medium-temperature thermal storage applications, demonstrating high heat release efficiency in practical scenarios.

As previously mentioned, it has a significantly higher thermal conductivity, 0.60 W m-1 K-1 [39], as compared to 0.15 W m-1 K-1 of P130. The key properties of both materials are summarized in Table 1.

To emphasize the role of the mutual interaction between the PCMs and the different internal structures, the same PCM volume was used in all samples to ensure complete immersion of the metal framework in the liquid phase. To guarantee meaningful comparisons across different configurations, all samples—including the reference case—were designed with the same internal volume. This approach ensured that the enhancement structures were fully embedded in the PCM and that the amount of material (both PCM and metal) remained consistent across tests. By fixing the volume, we avoided scenarios where part of the metallic structure would remain unutilized due to incomplete PCM coverage, which could have compromised the comparability of thermal performance. Consequently, 55 g of the organic PCM and 60 g of erythritol were used. However, this choice resulted in an energy storage imbalance, as the organic PCM can store approximately 12 kJ, while erythritol can reach around 20 kJ. It is important to note that this energy storage discrepancy does not affect the heat transfer analysis because the temperature distribution inside the samples is not influenced by the total energy capacity of the system (i.e., energy density capacity). Moreover, the reference sample, which contained no enhancement structure, was filled with the same volume of PCM as the structured samples, allowing for a direct assessment of the relative charging and discharging enhancements attributable solely to the geometry of the enhancement structures. A similar approach was also followed by Righetti et al. [34,37].

One of the main objectives of the present study is to investigate how different PCMs respond to the same thermal enhancement provided by metal structures under the same operating conditions. For this purpose, four test samples were considered all made of AlSi10Mg-0403 aluminum alloy via additive manufacturing. The use of additive manufacturing allows for the creation of complex, highly controlled geometric structures.

The thermal conductivity of the AlSi10Mg-0403 alloy was determined experimentally [38] using a Transient Hot Disk transient plane source TPS 2500S apparatus to measure thermal diffusivity. The test, repeated ten times, provided an average thermal diffusivity of 41.57 mm²/s at 20 °C, corresponding to an estimated thermal conductivity of approximately 96 W/m-1K-1.

Fig. 2 provides a schematic of the four samples, while Table 2 lists their main geometrical properties. A picture of the sample is presented in Fig. 3 . Each sample has a base area of 42 × 42 mm² and a total height of 60 mm, including a 12 mm-high base to ensure uniform heat distribution. One sample serves as a reference, containing only PCM without any internal structure. The other three samples feature a 40 mm-high periodic 3D structure based on a pyramidal cell. The three structured samples differ only in the arrangement of the aluminum structure, with periodic cell base sizes of 10, 20, and 40 mm. As the cell size increases, the number of ligaments decreases while their cross-section increases to maintain a constant porosity of 95 % (i.e., void volume divided by total volume). For convenience, these samples are referred to as "1," "2," and "4," for 10, 20, and 40 mm cell size, respectively, while the reference sample is labelled "r."

Fig. 4 presents the experimental test rig, which includes a heater block coupled to the sample using a thin layer of silicone heat transfer paste. The heater block, made of aluminum (thermal conductivity: 205 W/mK), is 20 mm high with the same base area as the samples (42 × 42 mm²). A central 8 mm diameter hole houses a 200 W/240 V electric cartridge heater controlled by a Variac. In this study, the supplied thermal power ranged from 30 to 70 W, corresponding to heat fluxes between 18.75 and 43.75 kW/m². The current and the voltage drop of the electrical resistance are measured independently. Thus, the electrical power supplied to the samples from the cartridge heater was calculated using Ohm’s law:
P
E L
=
Δ
V · I
where the current I was measured using a precision shunt resistor (0.0025 Ω ± 0.25 % at 25 °C), and ΔV was the voltage drop across the heater. The uncertainty on the electrical power, evaluated according the KIine and McClintock method [41], was estimated to be always below ±0.5 % of the reading.

To minimize heat losses to the environment, the heater block and the sample base were insulated with rock wool within a wooden enclosure and a high-conductivity silicone paste was used to minimize the thermal contact resistance to the sample. Heat losses were evaluated through numerical analysis and presented in a previously published study by the same research group [38], and were estimated to be around 1.5–1.7 W, which corresponds to 5–6 % of the total input power at 30 W. More in detail, the heat losses were calculated using a combined numerical and experimental approach. Heat losses to the surroundings were estimated through a calibration procedure with an aluminum block of identical dimensions to the investigated samples. The calibration involved inserting multiple thermocouples in different positions of the aluminum block to measure temperature changes during the heating process. The heat losses were determined by balancing the electrical power supplied to the block with the heat lost by natural convection and through the heating base. The heat flux was calculated by subtracting the estimated losses from the supplied electrical power. Additionally, a numerical simulation was performed using Ansys Fluent 18.2 to model the system's behavior, focusing on convection and heat transfer. This model helped predict natural convection losses and provided a boundary condition iteration process to match experimental data. The heat losses were found to increase proportionally with the sample's mean temperature.
To ensure consistent experimental conditions, all tests were conducted inside a climatic chamber set to a stable ambient temperature of 20 ± 0.2 °C.

As reported in Fig. 4, the temperature was recorded in multiple locations using calibrated against a certified PT1000 sensor T-type thermocouples. Each thermocouple provided temperature readings with an accuracy of ±0.1 °C (k = 2), connected to a K170 Ice Point Reference with a stability of ±0.005 °C and an accuracy of ±0.005 °C.

Thermocouples from t4 to t9 were embedded in the PCM to monitor the internal temperature distribution. Specifically:
-
t4 and t5 were placed near the lateral walls at 20 mm from the base (t4 at a corner, t5 in the middle of a side wall).
-
from t6 to t9 were positioned at the sample’s center at 1, 5, 20, and 35 mm from the base, respectively.

The thermocouples were positioned using pre-aligned supports that allowed reproducible placement with a spatial uncertainty estimated between 0.5–1 mm, which is comparable to the thermal sensing region of the thermocouples’ junction.

Additional thermocouples were positioned outside the PCM to assess heat dissipation, detect edge effects, and monitor whether the heater or structure reached critical temperatures. The highest temperature was recorded at the aluminum base via t3, inserted in a 1 mm diameter hole just 1 mm below the surface. Data were recorded at 1 Hz using a 34970A Agilent Data Acquisition System and elaborated using a dedicated interface built in Labview environment.

All experiments were conducted in a controlled environment with a temperature stability of ±0.2 °C. Repeatability tests were also performed by running the same test for each PCM (P130 and erythritol) under identical conditions using the 10 mm sample and a heat input of 50 W five times. The maximum deviation observed was ±4 % for the charging phase and ±3 % for the discharging phase, both in the case of P130. This slightly higher variability for P130 may be attributed to its shorter total test duration, which increases the relative impact of small timing fluctuations.

The experimental campaign comprised multiple tests, each including a charging and a discharging phase. The definition of the test was made according to previous experiments run on different materials [36,38], which allowed to establish a reliable and repeatable methodology to build a robust database of comparable test campaigns.

The charging phase began when the sample was uniformly at a temperature of 20 °C. A constant heat flux was applied via an electric cartridge heater, and the process continued until the entire PCM had melted and its temperature exceeded the melting point by at least 1 K, at which point the heater was turned off. Each sample was tested at different heat input levels: 30, 40, 50, 60, and 70 W, corresponding to heat fluxes of 18.75, 25, 31.25, 37.5, and 43.75 kW m-².

The discharging phase started immediately after charging and continued until all temperature readings within the PCM dropped below 40 °C. No external heat was supplied during this stage, meaning that the heat transfer occurred solely through natural convection with the surrounding ambient air at 20 °C. The test was deliberately stopped at 40 °C to avoid excessively long times to reach thermal equilibrium with the ambient, which would have provided limited additional insight given the study’s objectives.

The results are analyzed in terms of temperature distribution within the samples while the charging and discharging times are discussed for each sample individually and a qualitative comparison is also done.

Fig. 5 illustrates the evolution of the average PCM temperature within the 10 mm base size sample throughout the tests conducted at different thermal power levels. Fig. 4a presents the results for the P130, while Fig. 4b shows those for erythritol. As the electrical power input increases from 30 W to 70 W, the melting process accelerates, leading to a higher peak temperature and a faster charging rate, as expected. Moreover, the different melting temperature can be clearly identified, which for P130 is slightly higher as compared to erythritol. The 30 W test lasts proportionally longer in both materials compared to all other cases. The test ended when the entire PCM had melted and its temperature recorded by all the thermocouples exceeded the melting point listed in Table 1 by at least 1 K.

Throughout this test, certain regions of the material exhibit difficulty in absorbing heat, maintaining their solid state for extended periods and consequently prolonging the charging phase. When increasing the power from 30 W to 70 W, the test duration extends by a factor of 4.5 for P130 (moving from 16 min to 74 min) and approximately 2.5 for erythritol (moving from 29 min to 75 min). This observation leads to the conclusion that erythritol demonstrates lower sensitivity to variations in applied electrical power, potentially offering an advantage in scenarios where thermal input may undergo sudden or significant changes. For instance, in applications such as waste heat recovery systems or thermal energy storage, where power fluctuations are common due to dynamic operational conditions, erythritol's more stable response could ensure more reliable performance. Similarly, in industries dealing with intermittent or highly variable energy sources, such as solar or wind energy, erythritol's ability to maintain thermal stability despite power changes could provide greater efficiency and longer operational lifespan.

Fig. 6 compares the three 3D enhanced structured samples against the reference sample during the 50 W test. Once again, Fig. 6a shows the results for the organic material, while Fig. 6b displays the erythritol data. The test ended when the entire PCM had melted and its temperature recorded by all the thermocouples exceeded the melting point listed in Table 1 by at least 1 K.

In both materials, the reference sample requires more time to complete the charging process. In the case of erythritol, the reference sample takes approximately 15 % longer compared to all three structured samples, which exhibit similar charging times (47 min vs. around 42 min). Conversely, for the P130, the 40 mm base size structure achieves nearly a 30 % reduction in charging time compared to the reference sample. The 20 mm base size sample has a longer charging time, while the 10 mm base size sample shows a reduction of about 15 % relative to the reference (36 min vs. 32 min for the 10 mm, 30 for the 20 mm, 28 for the 40 mm). Unlike erythritol, where the structure type does not significantly impact the charging time, P130 performance appears to be influenced by the geometry of the structures.

To further illustrate this, Fig. 7 directly compares the two materials. For clarity, only the reference sample, the most densely structured sample (i.e. the one with the smallest base size, labelled as 1), and the sample with the largest base size (labelled as 4) are shown. The subscript "er" denotes erythritol, while "org" refers to the organic material. On average, the P130 undergoes phase transition in a shorter time. This is because, during the charging phase, it absorbs a lower amount of energy due to its lower density and latent heat of fusion (as mentioned, the volume is the same for both PCMs).

Additionally, as stated before, P130 melts at a slightly higher temperature, which is evident in the PCM's average temperature profile. Beyond these minor differences arising from intrinsic material properties, the comparison between the reference and structured samples confirms the previous observations: in erythritol, the presence of a structure reduces the charging time by approximately 20 % compared to the reference sample, with no significant dependence on structure type. In contrast, for the P130, the structure with the larger base size reduces the charging time by 15 %, aligning with the erythritol results, while the structure with the smaller base size achieves a greater reduction of approximately 30 %.

To better explain this behavior, the temperature distribution during individual tests is analyzed, as shown in Figs. 8 and 9
for P130 and erythritol, respectively. The results illustrate the temperature field measured by the thermocouples located along the center vertical line of the samples with 10 mm and 40 mm samples for both P130 and erythritol. As it can be seen in Fig. 4, the temperature probe t6 is positioned close to the heated base, followed by t7, t8 at the center of the sample, and t9 a few millimetres below the upper surface.

Focusing on t9, the temperature near the top surface is consistently the lowest, most likely due to its greater distance from the heat source. Initially, in P130 (Fig. 8a) it remains at 20 °C, consistent with the isothermal condition of the entire sample. It then increases almost linearly during the initial sensible heating phase, albeit with a lower slope compared to other regions, due to reduced heat flux. As phase change begins within the material, the t9 temperature rise slows down, eventually reaching a plateau around 110 °C. This plateau reflects the latent heat absorption occurring in other parts of the sample.

The temperature at t8, located at the geometric center of the sample, initially matches t9 during the isothermal stage. However, it starts to increase more rapidly due to a slightly higher heat input. Its temperature profile is similar, though the phase change plateau occurs at a higher temperature, around 115 °C. On average, across the entire heating process, t8 is approximately 8.3 °C warmer than t9.

In contrast, t6 and t7, located closer to the heated base, register the highest temperatures throughout the experiment. Their readings are almost identical, differing by less than 1 °C. Both sensors record temperatures approximately 4.7 °C higher than t8 on average. A plateau phase is also observed in this region, lasting about 5–8 min for the 10 mm sample and around 5 min for the 40 mm sample. This plateau is less defined, as the temperature measured by these thermocouples reflects a local average between subdomains at different states (e.g., superheated liquid, phase-changing material, and solid). Nevertheless, a marked slowdown in temperature rise is observed around 128 °C, the P130 melting temperature.

For erythritol (Fig. 9), the maximum temperature is again recorded by t6, the thermocouple nearest to the heated base. Starting from the initial 20 °C, its temperature increases rapidly due to the high heat flux, then reaches a plateau as the phase change begins locally. Compared to the organic PCM, this plateau is more pronounced, lasting over 10 min for the 10 mm sample, and slightly shorter for the 40 mm sample. This behavior is attributed to erythritol's higher thermal conductivity, which promotes more uniform heat distribution and synchronizes the phase change in the surrounding material.

Thermocouple t7, slightly above t6, records lower temperatures. In the 40 mm sample, its average temperature is 14 °C lower than t6 during the whole test lenght, and by the end of the test, even falls below the value recorded by t9. As in the case of P130, a clear temperature gradient is observed from the heated base toward the top surface: t8, at the center, shows a temperature approximately 9 °C (10 mm sample) and 16 °C (40 mm sample) lower than t6.

Interestingly, in the 40 mm erythritol sample, t9 records higher temperatures than t8 in the final stage. This can be attributed to the upward movement of hot, melted material from the bottom of the sample, which reaches and warms the top region. Around 30 min into the test, t9 exceeds 120 °C, indicating local phase change into the liquid state.

In both materials, heat transfer during phase change is governed by conduction and, to a lesser extent, convection. The similar density of the two PCMs does not provide a notable advantage for convective effects. However, the significantly higher thermal conductivity of erythritol promotes greater temperature uniformity, especially when used in combination with metallic structures.

Experimental data confirm this expectation: erythritol exhibits a lower overall temperature gradient during the heating process. In P130, the maximum temperature difference between the base and the top reaches 17 °C for the 10 mm sample and 19 °C for the 40 mm sample. In contrast, for erythritol, the gradient is reduced to 11 °C and 16 °C for the 10 mm and 40 mm samples, respectively. Notably, temperatures at t7, t8, and t9 in erythritol tend to converge and even overlap for extended durations, indicating improved thermal homogeneity.

These results reinforce previous findings reported in the literature: the design and optimization of enhanced surfaces must be tailored to the specific thermophysical properties of the PCM and the intended application context [35,36,38].

Nevertheless, further studies are essential to analyze phase change dynamics as a function of thermal conductivity and to determine whether this behavior is consistently observed in other families of PCMs. Additionally, exploring alternative geometries could help establish whether the optimal structure is among those tested or if improvements exceeding 15 % can be achieved, and whether the optimal configuration is also material-dependent. A geometry be considered optimal according to two key performance metrics: maximum charging time reduction and minimum internal temperature gradient during phase change.

The discharge phase, represented in Fig. 10 , compares the average PCM temperature in all studied samples. All samples undergo the same cooling process via natural convection with ambient air. The test starts immediately at the end of the charging phase, the average temperature of the sample was comparable but not identical since the charging phase was concluded only when all the thermocouples exceeded the melting by at least 1 K. It ended when the average temperature was below 40 °C.

In this phase, no significant differences are observed between individual samples, including those with metallic structures and the reference sample. This is likely because the natural convective heat transfer between the ambient and the sample through the external walls is so low (typically around 10–15 W m-1 K-1) that the heat can diffuse and distribute relatively homogeneously throughout the storage volume. Nevertheless, the sample with the smallest base size (10 mm) completes the discharge process in less time, while the other tests show comparable durations. However, there is a great key difference between P130 and erythritol: P130 solidifies with a negligible supercooling (<1 K), whereas the sugar alcohol exhibits significant supercooling, estimated to be around 18 K in the reference case. It is interesting to point out that the enhanced surfaces permit to mitigate the subcooling, which unfortunately remains greater than 10 K.

The significant supercooling observed in erythritol represents a critical limitation for its application in latent heat thermal energy storage systems. A supercooling range of 10–18 K, as recorded in this study, can substantially delay the onset of crystallization during the discharge phase. This delay reduces the effective temperature range over which latent heat is released, thereby decreasing the amount of usable thermal energy that can be recovered in each cycle. In comparison, many organic PCMs, such as paraffin waxes and fatty acids, typically exhibit supercooling values below 5 K [42], while erythritol is consistently reported to show higher values, often exceeding 10–15 K [43,44]. This characteristic limits its practical deployment unless specific countermeasures, such as the incorporation of nucleating agents or the use of thermally conductive structural materials, are implemented.

These results highlight one more time the necessity of investigating each PCM individually, since the results obtained for other materials or under similar conditions do not appear to be universally applicable [34,37,38].

Moreover, it clearly appears that the discharging phase, without any external cooling, represents the bottleneck of the process, even when the enhanced surfaces are used. As highlighted also by Righetti et al. [38], a proper design should consider the whole process to minimize the cycle time and allows for an efficient use of the LTES.

During the discharging phase, no significant differences were observed between the various structures for either P130 or erythritol. This behavior is attributed to the low-intensity heat flux applied during cooling, which is governed by forced convection with ambient air. Under these conditions, the thermal response of all samples becomes nearly uniform, and the internal temperature gradient measured within the PCM remained below 1 °C throughout the process. Given the temperature measurement uncertainty (±0.1 °C), such small variations are not sufficient to justify a comparative analysis, as they could lead to misleading conclusions.

In the case of erythritol, a more pronounced subcooling was observed in the reference sample (i.e., without internal metallic structures), with the minimum recorded temperature reaching approximately 98 °C, compared to about 104 °C in the structured samples. This effect was consistent across all thermocouples within the PCM, indicating that it is not attributable to a localized phenomenon. These results suggest that the presence of metallic structures—regardless of their specific geometry—can help reduce subcooling by promoting a more homogeneous temperature distribution and potentially enhancing nucleation during the phase change process.

This study experimentally evaluated the performance of two organic PCMs, erythritol and P130, with comparable melting points (∼125 °C), but significantly different thermal properties, particularly in terms of thermal conductivity and supercooling behavior. Both materials were tested under identical working conditions using a set of three 3D-printed periodic aluminum structures with varying geometries but the same amount of metal (plus one reference), in order to assess the influence of material properties and structural enhancements on heat transfer during charge and discharge cycles. The findings provide the following insights for the design of latent thermal energy storage systems tailored to medium-high temperature waste heat recovery.
1.
The presence of aluminium enhancements significantly improved charging performance for both PCMs. Erythritol showed a consistent ∼20 % reduction in charging time across all geometries, while P130 was more sensitive to structure, reaching up to ∼30 % time reduction with the 10 mm geometry, likely due to its lower thermal conductivity.
2.
Temperature distribution inside the PCM was strongly affected by the material’s thermal conductivity. Erythritol enabled more uniform heating due to its higher conductivity, whereas P130 developed steeper temperature gradients, which slowed down the charging process.
3.
During the discharging phase, both materials exhibited similar overall behavior in terms of timing, but erythritol showed significant supercooling (up to 15 K), which persisted even with enhanced structures. P130, on the other hand, displayed no measurable supercooling, resulting in more predictable discharge performance. Interestingly, the reference sample with erythritol showed a more pronounced subcooling effect (∼6 K lower than the structured cases), consistently across all thermocouples, suggesting that the presence of metallic structures—regardless of geometry—may help reduce subcooling by promoting nucleation.

From a practical standpoint, these findings indicate that P130’s strong sensitivity to geometry could be leveraged to design more compact and cost-effective LTES systems by optimizing structure dimensions. In contrast, while erythritol is low-cost and biodegradable, its supercooling behavior may require additional components (e.g., nucleating agents) to ensure repeatable performance. This highlights a trade-off between thermal performance, material cost, and system complexity that must be evaluated during PCM selection.

The results confirm that enhanced surfaces are essential for improving the thermal response in LTES systems, as both materials exhibited a reduction in overall charging time. However, the optimal enhancement geometry (terms of both charging time reduction and internal temperature uniformity) must be tailored to the specific PCM, since the two materials responded differently to the tested structures: P130 showed a continuous decrease in melting time with decreasing cell base size. While the 10 mm geometry proved optimal in this study, smaller cell sizes (e.g., 5 mm) could offer further improvements if manufacturable, although trade-offs such as increased production cost and potential structural fragility must be carefully considered. On the other hand, erythritol was less affected by variations in enhancement geometry so an optimal one could not be identified.

Giulia Righetti: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Dario Guarda: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Luca Doretti: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology. Claudio Zilio: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Francesca Martelletto: Validation, Data curation. Andrea Dolfi: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Giuseppe Travaglini: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Simone Mancin: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
